summary: Gruber methodically builds a legal and moral case that the individuals who found Apple's prototype fourth-generation iPhone committed theft under California law by failing to make reasonable efforts to return it, and that Gizmodo knowingly purchased stolen property. He walks through the California penal and civil codes governing found property, dismantles the finders' claimed attempts to return the phone, and highlights Gizmodo's contradictory behavior — publishing the device with certainty it was Apple's while simultaneously claiming they didn't know it was real. He closes by condemning Gizmodo's decision to publish the personal identity of the Apple engineer who lost the phone as a gratuitous, sociopathic act irrelevant to the story.
key_points: Under California law, keeping found property without making reasonable efforts to return it constitutes theft — and the finders had multiple obvious avenues (the bar, mail, Facebook, an Apple Store) they chose not to use

Gizmodo's defense that they didn't know the phone was genuine is undermined by their own headline ('This Is Apple's Next iPhone') published 12 hours before they claimed uncertainty, and by their editing of Brian Lam's response to Apple's legal counsel after the fact
The DVT (design verification test) designation on the prototype's barcode indicates this unit very closely resembles what Apple plans to ship, making it an extremely late-stage device
Apple routinely fields pre-production iPhones for cellular network testing, making occasional loss inevitable — but loss does not make subsequent sale legal
Publishing the Apple engineer's name and personal details served no journalistic purpose and was purely cruel
money_quotes: A phone that is allowed to leave campus is a phone that can be lost or stolen. A phone that is lost or stolen can wind up in the wrong hands. And we all know Murphy's Law.
Even if you take their account at face value, it is clear the individuals who sold this unit to Gizmodo made no serious attempt to return the phone.
Thus, even if the phone originally came into their hands by being lost, once they made no 'reasonable and just' effort to return it and instead began trying to sell it, it became stolen.
But they could have simply put the phone in a bubble wrap envelope and mailed it to 1 Infinite Loop.
That everyone else is as dishonest as they are. Taking it to an Apple Store, asking for a manager, and handing it over would have put the phone back in Apple's hands.
It simply boggles my mind the stakes they have effectively wagered that Apple will not pursue this legally.
Publishing the name, photographs, and personal information of the Apple engineer who lost the phone is irrelevant to the story. It was the dick move to end all dick moves.
There is no interest served by outing him other than taking sociopathic glee in making a public spectacle of someone who made a very serious but honest mistake.
key_insight: What Gizmodo framed as a lucky find and journalistic scoop was, under California law and basic ethical standards, the purchase of stolen property followed by the gratuitous public humiliation of the person who lost it.
